title: SEGMENT 17 — Cross-species translatability: the central bet

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Cross-species translatability: the central bet

We've now toured three model organisms across half a century, and the recurring story has been the same: a mechanism discovered in worm or fly or fish turns out to be conserved across animals, including humans.
Apoptosis in worms.
Hox genes in flies.
Circadian clocks in flies.
Patterning genes in fish.
Insulin signaling and aging.
RNAi.
The list goes on.

The central bet of model-organism biology is that what's true in worm is often true in mouse is often true in human; not always, but enough of the time to make the model approach work for fifty years.
Let me make this concrete.
Cases where the translation worked beautifully.
The Hox gene cluster of vertebrates does the same thing as the Hox cluster of flies — they pattern the anterior-posterior body axis using the same genes in the same chromosomal order.
The clock genes of flies (period, timeless, etc.) have direct mammalian homologs running the same molecular oscillator.
The cell-death genes of worms (ced-3, ced-9, etc.) are the homologs of the human caspases and Bcl-2 proteins.
The Wnt, hedgehog, Notch, and BMP signaling pathways were largely worked out in flies and fish, and they are the same pathways operating in human development and disease.
Cases where the translation is partial.
Drosophila has only a single Hox cluster, while vertebrates have four — duplicated and divergent.
The fly has no adaptive immune system, while mammals do.
Zebrafish have a partially functional p53 pathway that differs in important ways from mammalian p53.
The TGF-beta superfamily has many more members in vertebrates than in flies, and not all of them have direct invertebrate counterparts.
Where the translation is partial, you have to be careful — the fly experiment gives you a hypothesis that needs to be validated in mouse before you trust it in human.
Cases where the translation fails.
Aging in mammals involves stem-cell exhaustion, cellular senescence, and immunoaging — processes that have no direct worm equivalent.
Many neurological diseases involve specific neuronal cell types that don't exist in flies.
The mammalian gut microbiome plays an enormous role in physiology with no real fly or worm equivalent.
When the failure happens, model-organism work can mislead — a mechanism that looked critical in worm or fly turns out to be irrelevant or different in mammals.
The analogy: cross-species translatability is the central bet of model-organism biology.
What's true in worm is often true in mouse is often true in human; not always, but enough of the time to make the model approach work for fifty years.
It's a probabilistic bet, not a guarantee.
Each individual finding in a model organism is a hypothesis about human biology, not a fact.
The hypothesis has to be tested in increasingly complex systems — first mouse, then human cells, eventually clinical trials — before it becomes therapeutic.
The strategic value is that model organisms generate the hypotheses cheaply and quickly.
The fly or worm or fish points you at a gene, a pathway, a mechanism.
Then you go to mouse to validate.
Then to human cells.
Then to patients.
Without the model organism work upstream, you wouldn't know which of the twenty thousand human genes to investigate.
The model organisms are the search engine, the prioritization layer, the funnel.
The big idea: the model-organism enterprise is a probability machine.
It feeds the rest of biomedicine a stream of high-probability candidate mechanisms drawn from cheap, fast, tractable animals.
Some of the candidates fail when tested in mammals.
Most succeed at least partially.
The success rate is high enough that fifty years of work has built much of modern biomedicine on this foundation.
It is, on net, the best bet any field has ever made.
